justification: The agent confirms all standard ADR sections are present (Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Considered) and maps each element back to specific lines. Crash-recovery lock behavior is explicitly addressed (line 35 TCP keepalive/statement timeout callout, plus domain rule and acceptance criterion for crash recovery). All three alternatives (Redis SETNX, ZooKeeper, heartbeat table) are accounted for at lines 25-29. Context explains the multi-node expansion problem (lines 9-11). No invented information — the agent cross-references MJ's note and flags only what was already present. The handoff brief demonstrates the ADR is readable as a standalone document.

observations.text: Systematic cross-reference table mapping MJ's note to specific ADR lines provides clear traceability
Crash-recovery risk is not just mentioned but made actionable with TCP keepalive/statement timeout guidance
All three alternatives confirmed present without inventing additional ones
Handoff brief adds valuable operational detail (acceptance criteria, open questions) beyond the basic ADR review

Agent reviewed an existing ADR rather than writing one from scratch — output is a review/confirmation rather than the ADR itself, though the rubric criteria are still satisfied since the ADR content is validated

agent_output_excerpt:
The ADR already exists and is well-structured. Let me review it against MJ's decision note to confirm completeness and accuracy.

The existing ADR at `docs/adr/adr-postgresql-advisory-locks-for-batch-coordination.md` is already written and covers all of MJ's inputs:

| MJ's Note | ADR Coverage |
|---|---|
| Decision: `pg_try_advisory_lock` | Lines 14-15 |
| Rationale: no new dependency, sufficient load | Lines 19-21 |
| Trade-offs: no visibility, no TTL, crash semantics | Lines 32-35 |
